data.title: Nordson outlines Q3 2025 sales guidance of $710M–$750M amid strategic portfolio actions and robust ATS demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nordson Corporation (NDSN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Sundaram Nagarajan opened by highlighting increasing order entry and backlog momentum, stating that "we started the second quarter with increasing momentum in order entry and backlog, enabling us to outperform the midpoint of our sales and earnings guidance." He noted strong performance in Advanced Technology system and parts sales, ongoing customer demand in semiconductor and selected electronics, as well as solid growth in nonwovens systems, medical fluid components, and precision agriculture. Nagarajan reported that the Atrion integration continues to "perform above our valuation model expectations."
  • He announced the agreement to divest select product lines in the Medical Contract Manufacturing business, explaining, "exiting these product lines will increase focus on higher value growth opportunities within the $800 million Medical and Fluid Solutions segment, namely within our growing portfolio of proprietary medical components."
  • Nagarajan emphasized operational excellence, sharing that "operational excellence during the quarter drove strong profit performance resulting in 32% overall EBITDA margins."
  • CFO Daniel Hopgood stated, "second quarter fiscal 2025 sales were $683 million up 5% from the prior year second quarter sales of $651 million." EBITDA adjusted for restructuring and integration costs was $217 million or 32% of sales, marking a 7% increase from the prior year. Hopgood added, "the impact of tariffs was not material to the company's operating financial performance in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Nordson expects third quarter fiscal 2025 sales to be in the range of $710 million to $750 million. Third quarter adjusted earnings are forecasted to be between $2.55 and $2.75 per diluted share.
  • Nagarajan described the guidance as "in line with the full year expectations we set at the beginning of our fiscal year and confirmed in our Q1 earnings call."
  • The company noted that "current tariff levels" are manageable and does not expect policies to have a material impact on results.

Financial Results

  • Hopgood reported sales of $683 million, a 5% increase from the prior year. Gross profit was $374 million, representing 55% of sales. EBITDA, adjusted for restructuring and integration costs, was $217 million or 32% of sales.
  • Net income totaled $112 million or $1.97 per share on a GAAP basis. Adjusted earnings per share were $2.42, a 3% increase from the prior year's $2.34.
  • Segment results included Industrial Precision Solution sales of $319 million (down 8%), Medical and Fluid Solution sales of $203 million (up 20%, with Atrion contributing $51 million), and Advanced Technology Solutions sales of $161 million (up 18%).
  • At quarter end, cash on hand was approximately $130 million and net debt was about $2.1 billion, resulting in a leverage ratio of 2.4 times. Free cash flow for the quarter was $103 million.
  • The company repurchased $85 million in shares and paid $44 million in dividends during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Michael Halloran, Baird: Asked about trends through the rest of the year and sustainability in ATS and Medical. Nagarajan explained that "ATS in general, pretty good feel pretty good about it going playing out through the rest of the year," while Medical is seeing "Atrion contributing to growth, very solid performance above our model expectations." He added that destocking in interventional products is lessening.
  • Halloran also inquired about the Contract Manufacturing divestiture. Hopgood stated, "this is roughly 4% of our year-to-date sales in the Medical segment" and estimated the move would be "roughly a 100 basis points accretive to our Medical segment margins going forward post-sale."
  • Andrew Buscaglia, BNP Paribas: Asked about ATS volatility and order improvement. Nagarajan responded, "this is a business inherently lumpy...but overall...this is the part of the cycle where it is starting to grow," with strong order entry supporting the outlook. Hopgood noted that "most of the growth we're seeing right now is really with our Asian customers."
  • Saree Boroditsky, Jefferies: Asked about margin performance in ATS. Nagarajan said, "we have adjusted the cost structure such that, in a downturn, the margins would be lower than where it is today...but it's not going to be a place where we have significantly lower margin performance like seven years, five years ago."
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: Probed on Atrion's upside. Nagarajan responded, "we're integrating nicely. We're certainly performing well commercially. They've got one new product out there that we're doing really well."
  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson: Inquired about tariff exposure. Hopgood replied, "tariffs are very manageable. And as we stated, really had no material impact on our second quarter results."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on segment growth consistency, margin sustainability, and the impact of global tariffs, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone as they acknowledged improvements but sought specifics on volatility and risk.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing strong order momentum and operational execution. Nagarajan repeatedly highlighted, "we feel really good about the trends we see" and "order entry and backlog are building and are supportive."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism regarding guidance and order recovery to a more assertive and confident stance regarding growth, especially in ATS and Medical.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance for the next quarter remains unchanged from Q1, with management reiterating the sales and earnings range previously set.
  • Q2 reported stronger order entry and backlog growth, particularly in ATS and Medical segments, compared to Q1’s focus on order acceleration and backlog increase.
  • Segment performance in Q2 showed improvement in ATS and Medical, with particular emphasis on Atrion’s contribution exceeding expectations, whereas Q1 highlighted operational resilience amid lower organic sales.
  • Analyst questions in Q2 focused more on the sustainability of growth trends and margin improvements versus Q1’s focus on recovery timing and segment realignment.
  • Management expressed higher confidence in segment outlooks and operational adaptability in Q2, compared to the more cautious approach in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing geopolitical and trade policy uncertainties, particularly regarding tariffs, were cited as a challenge. Nagarajan stated, "we remain agile in our action plans, knowing there is still plenty of market uncertainty due to tariffs."
  • Weaker customer demand in select industrial system sales persists, though sequential improvement was noted.
  • Management implemented targeted restructuring to adjust cost structure in weaker businesses, expecting $50 million in ongoing annual benefits by 2026.
  • The potential impact of tariffs on end-market demand remains a key risk, though current exposure is described as manageable.

Final Takeaway

Nordson’s management highlighted continued momentum in order entry and backlog, particularly in Advanced Technology and Medical segments, supporting the reaffirmed Q3 guidance range for sales and earnings. Strategic actions, including the divestiture of select Medical Contract Manufacturing product lines and strong operational execution, position the company for improved long-term growth and margin accretion. Management remains confident in navigating ongoing market and policy uncertainties, underpinned by a robust balance sheet and a focus on high-value proprietary products and operational agility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IDC chops 2025 smartphone forecast amid economic uncertainty, including tariffs
data.description:
Research firm IDC cut its forecast for smartphone shipments this year, citing significant economic uncertainty stemming from a number of factors, including tariffs.

“Since April 2nd, the smartphone industry has faced a whirlwind of uncertainty,” IDC senior research director Nabila Popal said in a statement, referring to the Trump administration's “Liberation Day” tariff announcement.

“While current exemptions on smartphones have offered temporary relief, the looming possibility of broader tariffs presents a serious risk,” Popal added. “Recent signals from the US administration on potential tariffs hikes on smartphones manufactured outside the US further complicate long-term strategic planning for OEMs. Smartphone vendors — particularly those shipping to the US — must now navigate complex geopolitics alongside ongoing supply chain diversification efforts.”

IDC now expects worldwide smartphone shipments — which includes those made by Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and others — to rise just 0.6% year-over-year to 1.24B units, down from a prior view of 2.3% growth.

Popal said that tariff concerns on countries such as India, Vietnam and China could wind up posing “a serious downside risk to the current U.S. market outlook.”

Delving deeper, IDC said that the U.S. and China are largely responsible for the 0.6% growth, but China is expected to see 3% year-over-year growth, due largely to government subsidies. This is expected to largely benefit Android (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) devices, while Apple could see increased competition from companies like Huawei and other Chinese smartphone manufacturers.

Apple is expected to see a 1.9% year-over-year decline in iPhone shipments in 2025, IDC said, citing increased competition, an economic slowdown and the fact that Chinese government subsidies are capped at 6,000 yuan.

While Apple's iPhone shipments are expected to decline, the upcoming 618 shopping festivals in China, along with recent increases in trade-in discounts and the upcoming iPhone 17 release later this year could boost demand, IDC added.

data.title: Market Voices: Trump advisor sees tariffs resuming, Musk leaves DOGE
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's daily roundup of statements and remarks by newsmakers that could move markets, sectors or individual stocks.

-Trump economic advisor Kevin Hassett, who serves as director of the National Economic Council, said he expects the Trump administration's aggressive use of tariffs to resume, despite a federal trade court ruling against the initiative on Wednesday. 

“The fact is that there are measures that we can take with different numbers that we can start right now. There are different approaches that would take a couple of months to put these in place,” Hassett said in an interview, according to CNBC. “We’re not planning to pursue those right now because we’re very, very confident that this ruling is incorrect.”

-Tesla CEO (NASDAQ:TSLA) Elon Musk bid adieu to his controversial government job as head of DOGE, adding that he expects the cost-cutting initiative to continue after his departure. Under federal guidelines, Musk was considered a special government employee and could only work for up to 130 days, or May 30.

"As my scheduled time as a Special Government Employee comes to an end, I would like to thank President @realDonaldTrump for the opportunity to reduce wasteful spending. The @DOGE mission will only strengthen over time as it becomes a way of life throughout the government," Musk posted on X on Wednesday night.

-Vice President J.D. Vance said the Trump administration doesn't view stablecoins as a threat to the U.S. dollar.

“In this administration, we do not think that stablecoins threaten the integrity of the U.S. dollar. Quite the opposite,” Vance said during a speech at Bitcoin 2025 on Wednesday, according to CNBC. “We view them as a force multiplier of our economic might.”

-The Federal Reserve is expecting the job market to weaken amid escalating tariffs, according to the minutes of its policy meeting in early May. 

“Participants assessed that there was a risk that the labor market would weaken in coming months, that considerable uncertainty surrounded the outlook for the labor market, and that outcomes would depend importantly on the evolution of trade policy as well as other government policies," according to the FOMC meeting minutes.

data.title: Bath & Body Works outlines strategy to drive 1%-3% sales growth in 2025 while advancing digital and international expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bath & Body Works (BBWI) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Daniel Heaf, Chief Executive Officer, opened the call by emphasizing the company's strong foundation and its position in home fragrance and beauty, highlighting more than 50,000 associates, approximately 1,900 North American stores, a predominantly US-based supply chain, and a loyalty program with around 39 million members. Heaf signaled a shift toward accelerating growth by putting the consumer at the center, stating, "We see a clear opportunity to transform Bath & Body Works to accelerate our growth, deepen our customer connection, and continue evolving for the future." Heaf flagged digital improvements, updated packaging, new distribution channels, and international expansion as key levers for growth.

  • Heaf underscored recent successes: "Our Disney collaboration and Everyday Luxuries are great examples of what happens when we listen to our customers, build coveted products, tell emotional stories and deliver it all seamlessly across all our channels."

  • Eva Boratto, Chief Financial Officer, remarked, "The energy level here at BBW is high and associates are eager to accelerate growth under [Daniel's] leadership." Boratto noted Q1 net sales up 3% and earnings per diluted share of $0.49, both at the high end of guidance. She cited innovation, strong loyalty program engagement, and disciplined cost management as major drivers.

Outlook

  • Boratto reaffirmed full year 2025 net sales growth guidance of 1% to 3% and earnings per share guidance of $3.25 to $3.60. She explained, "Building on our Q1 outperformance...we believe we are well-positioned to absorb the tariffs at current levels. If there are material changes in future tariffs, we will revisit our guidance."

  • Second quarter net sales are expected to be flat to up 2%, with gross profit rate at approximately 41% and SG&A rate around 30%. Q2 earnings per diluted share are forecasted between $0.33 and $0.38.

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q1 reached $1.4 billion, up 2.9% year over year, marking the strongest underlying sales growth since 2021. U.S. and Canadian store sales totaled $1.1 billion while direct net sales were $250 million. International sales contributed $64 million, representing about 5% of total net sales and up 10.1% year over year.

  • Gross profit rate rose to 45.4%, up 160 basis points. Operating income was $209 million, or 14.7% of net sales. Inventory ended Q1 up 7%, slightly above plan due to tariffs and strategic pull forwards.

  • The company returned $43 million to shareholders via dividends and repurchased 4.3 million shares for $135 million in Q1.

Q&A

  • Simeon Siegel, BMO Capital Markets, asked Heaf about what attracted him to Bath & Body Works and early observations. Heaf responded that the brand's strong emotional connection and business fundamentals set the stage for growth, emphasizing a consumer-centric approach.

  • Matthew Boss, J.P. Morgan, queried about building blocks for future growth and required investments. Heaf stated, "We will be guided by a clear and compelling vision...We will focus on fewer, bolder priorities, and we will target opportunities to accelerate the growth through consistent and repeatable growth drivers."

  • Lorraine Hutchinson, Bank of America, inquired about marketing plans and international expansion. Heaf said marketing would become less about promotions and more about storytelling, with international focus on select priority markets and tailored business models.

  • Alex Straton, Morgan Stanley, asked about growth expectations for core categories and changes to the semiannual sale. Boratto explained semiannual sale timing would shift to amplify Father’s Day and align with market trends, with marketing and product offering enhancements.

  • Ike Boruchow, Wells Fargo, questioned guidance amid tariff uncertainties. Boratto stated that if tariffs had remained at initial levels, "we would have been increasing our guidance today."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed probing questions on growth levers, marketing, tariffs, and category outlooks, with a generally positive but measured tone, seeking clarity on sustainability and execution.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, with Boratto highlighting, "I'm proud of our Q1 performance and energized by the opportunity to accelerate our growth in the future." In the Q&A, Heaf acknowledged his early tenure and the need to listen and learn, reflecting a collaborative and cautiously optimistic stance.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from a focus on establishing growth momentum to outlining actionable strategic priorities, while analysts continued to focus on growth drivers, innovation, and risk factors.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategic focus shifted from building momentum under previous CEO Gina Boswell to a new consumer-centered philosophy under Daniel Heaf, with a focus on digital, packaging, distribution, and international.

  • Guidance for 2025 net sales growth and EPS remained consistent with prior quarter, but Q1 outperformance was highlighted as evidence of execution.

  • Analysts’ questions this quarter concentrated more heavily on digital initiatives, marketing strategy, and international expansion, compared to the previous quarter’s focus on product innovation and loyalty growth.

  • Management’s confidence in innovation and cost discipline persisted, but new leadership brought a tone of actionable change and willingness to revisit strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited tariff-related inventory increases and ongoing macroeconomic challenges but emphasized mitigation strategies and a predominantly US-based supply chain.

  • Analysts raised concerns about tariff impact, sustainability of growth, and effectiveness of marketing and category expansion efforts.

Final Takeaway

Bath & Body Works delivered Q1 results at the high end of guidance, driven by innovation, successful collaborations, and a robust loyalty program. The company is maintaining its full-year outlook, with new CEO Daniel Heaf introducing a consumer-centered growth strategy focused on digital enhancements, packaging, new distribution, and international expansion. Management highlighted disciplined cost control and proactive tariff mitigation, positioning the company to achieve its 2025 sales and earnings targets while pursuing strategic transformation and deeper customer engagement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zeo Energy skyrockets on deal to buy solar thermal specialist Heliogen
data.description:
Zeo Energy (NASDAQ:ZEO) +94.3% in Thursday's trading after saying it agreed to acquire clean energy technology company Heliogen (OTCQX:HLGN) in an all-stock deal valued at ~$10M.

Under the deal terms, Heliogen (OTCQX:HLGN) shareholders will receive Zeo (NASDAQ:ZEO) common stock valued at ~$10M in the aggregate, based on a price of $1.5859/share.

Zeo (ZEO) said the acquisition will lead to the creation of a new division focused on long-duration energy generation and storage for commercial and industrial-scale facilities, including artificial intelligence and cloud computing data centers.

"Through this acquisition, we believe that Zeo will be able to accelerate our vision of serving energy consumers across the spectrum - from residential rooftops to larger-scale industrial solar and storage applications to build an energy platform at scale," Zeo Energy (ZEO) CEO Tim Bridgewater said.

data.title: MAHA report under attack for alleged fake sources, misinterpretations
data.description:
A report issued a week ago by the Make America Healthy Again Commission that heavily attacked overmedicalization, poor diet, and vaccine schedules as factors behind childhood diseases is now facing its own scrutiny amid accusations that some citations included in the assessment are made up. 

The MAHA report contains references to more than 500 studies and other sources used to back up arguments. However, seven of those sources don't exist, according to reporting from NOTUS. 

One such study dealing with anxiety and adolescents included was published in JAMA Pediatrics listing Katherine Keyes as the first author.  NOTUS reached Keyes who told the online publication that she wasn't involved with that article.

"The paper cited is not a real paper that I or my colleagues were involved with," Keyes said. "We've certainly done research on this topic, but did not publish a paper in JAMA Pediatrics on this topic with that co-author group, or with that title."

NOTUS added that a link to the article in the report is broken and although the citation lists the edition it appeared in, a look at that edition doesn't show the article in it.

One part of the report discusses "corporate capture of media" and provides citations for two studies it claims provides evidence that direct-to-consumer ads has led to a rise in children's prescriptions for ADHD and depression. 

Those citations lead to table of content pages for editions of their respective journals where those studies don't exist.

NOTUS also faulted the MAHA report for how it interpreted certain studies. For example, the assessment cites a review paper that claims that therapy alone is as or more effective than drugs. But one of the statisticians who worked on the paper told NOTUS that conclusion is wrong because their research didn't try to compare therapy’s effectiveness to medications.

data.title: Rates can come down if inflation resumes trend down, labor stays strong, Chicago Fed's Goolsbee says
data.description:
If inflation trends down, as it was before the April 2 tariffs, and the economy remains at full employment, then interest rates can come down, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said on Thursday at the Mackinac Policy Conference.

"If you can get the dust out of the air, I believe what's under there is a strong dual-mandate economy," he said. The dual mandate is the Federal Reserve's charge from Congress to preserve price stability and maximum employment.

As it stands now, traders price in a 97.9% chance that the Federal Open Market Committee will keep the federal funds rate target range at 4.25%-4.50% at its June 17-18 meeting, according to the CME FedWatch tool. For the September meeting, the probability of a 25-basis point cut rises to 51.2%, up from a 9.4% probability a month ago.

"Tariffs, for sure, are much more impactful on the Midwest," especially the Chicago Fed's district, which skews more toward agriculture and manufacturing than the nation as a whole, Goolsbee said.

"Imported goods are only 11% of the U.S. economy," he said. But there are three clear ways tariffs can "jump out of their lane" — retaliatory tariffs, if they bleed into domestic manufacturing, or if people change their behavior. 

That could pose the potential for inflation to resurge as the economy stagnates, what's often called "stagflation."

"Stagflation is the toughest scenario for a central bank, because there's no playbook," Goolsbee said. That can put the two sides of the mandate into conflict. "This is not 1970s-style stagflation."

He also stressed that an independent central bank is critical. "If politics gets to control the interest rate, then inflation is coming back," he said. President Donald Trump has long been voicing his view that the Fed should cut rates to encourage growth. Also, Richard Nixon and Lyndon B. Johnson had pressured the central bank to lower rates.

Paul Volcker, the Fed chair from 1979 to 1987, had jacked up the federal funds rate to 20% to break inflation that had surged in the late 1970s and early '80s, he pointed out. 

"COVID was by far the weirdest recession we ever had," Goolsbee said. That was followed by the "weirdest recovery we've ever had."

Housing inflation has been persistent, though it has started to come down. "If you look at the relative price of housing, the price of goods vs. the price of housing, it has been an extreme case in the last couple of years," he observed. That has made the relative price of housing, compared with other goods, "a lot more expensive."

data.title: Inspira stock soars on initiating global commercial rollout of ART100 system
data.description:
• Inspira Technologies (NASDAQ:IINN) has announced a strategic shift to accelerate the commercialization of its U.S. FDA cleared INSPIRA™ ART100 system, while maintaining the development and regulatory pathways of its portfolio of products.
  • This development follows the initial clinical implementation of the ART100 system in medical centers in the U.S., where the device has been used to treat patients in real-world clinical settings.
  • Based on the current ongoing discussions, deliveries are expected to begin in the second half of 2025, subject to logistical and regulatory alignment, and the finalization of commercial and legal terms.
  • "We recognize the immediate market potential for our ART100 system. This approach seeks to maximize shareholder value in the near and long term." said Dagi Ben Noon, CEO of Inspira Technologies.
  • IINN is +16.3% to $0.50.

data.title: VivoPower receives revised non-binding proposal from Energi for 51% stake in Tembo at $200M
data.description:
• VivoPower International (NASDAQ:VVPR) has received a revised non-binding proposal from Energi Holdings to acquire a 51% controlling stake in its subsidiary Tembo e-LV B.V.
  • The proposed deal values Tembo at an enterprise value of $200M, with adjustments to the equity purchase price based on net debt and other customary factors.
  • Energi supports Tembo’s planned merger with Cactus Acquisition Corp. 1 (OTC:CCTSF) and may roll its 51% stake into the combined entity post-transaction.
  • VivoPower would retain a significant shareholding in Tembo if the business combination proceeds.
  • Proceeds from the potential sale would be used to optimize VivoPower’s capital structure, including debt repayment and potentially returning surplus funds to shareholders.
  • VVPR shares up 7% on Thursday.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Boeing to resume deliveries to China next month; CEO aims to ramp up MAX production
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) +3.1% in Thursday's trading after CEO Kelly Ortberg said China is set to resume taking deliveries of the company's aircraft in June, after halting deliveries in April in retaliation to U.S. tariffs.

"China has now indicated... they're going to take deliveries. The first deliveries will be next month," Ortberg said at a Bernstein conference.

Boeing (NYSE:BA) has been paying tariffs on imported components from Italy and Japan for its wide-body Dreamliner planes, which are made in South Carolina, Ortberg said, adding that much of the tariffs can be recouped when the planes are exported again.

The CEO also said he wants to raise production of 737 MAX jets from the current monthly rate in the mid-30s to 47/month by the end of this year, and expects to reach monthly production of its best-selling plane to 42 by mid-year.

Boeing (BA) 737 production currently is capped at 38/month, a limit imposed by the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration last year after a midair accident involving a door plug that was not secured at the factory put the company's safety and production quality program under added scrutiny.
